frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Was talking to my sister today and we got nostalgic about Amma's cooking. Remember how she'd never let us eat store-bought anything? Every single meal, fresh and piping hot, even after a long day! It wasn't just food, it was her love, poured into every dish. I sometimes wonder how she managed, never complaining. Makes you feel like, no matter how much we try, we can never match that selfless dedication, can we? That's what a mother's love truly is.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment glorifies the mother's daily cooking and domestic labor as an ultimate expression of love and self-sacrifice, implicitly setting an unattainable standard for other women (including the speaker herself as a working woman) and implying that their efforts, or reliance on conveniences, are somehow less valuable or 'not the same.' This aligns with the 'labor erasure' and 'standard-setting' surface manifestations of the frame.
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 38. Married. A working woman in Bengaluru. You found out last week that your husband has been texting another woman from his office. He says it is nothing serious and has promised to stop. You are on a long phone call with your sister. You have decided to stay in the marriage. But in describing what happened you are directing almost all your anger at the other woman and almost none at your husband. You are telling your sister what kind of woman she must be to text a married man and what women like her want.
